entries.title: Viridarium Chymicum — Engraved Title Page
entries.creator: Daniel Stolcius
entries.summary:
The engraved title page of Daniel Stolcius's *Viridarium Chymicum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1624) — the architectural gateway to the 'Chymical Garden.'

## Iconography
A printed letterpress cartouche bearing the title — *Viridarium Chymicum figuris cupro incisis adornatum* — set within an engraved frame and flanked by two standing statues on plinths. The figure at left, labelled HERMES, holds a celestial sphere and caduceus-like staff; the figure at right, labelled PARACELSVS, stands as the modern master of the art. The imprint names M. Daniel Stolcius de Stolcenberg, Bohemian, and the publisher Lucas Jennis, with the date 1624. This is a title leaf, not a numbered emblem.

## Significance
By framing the book between Hermes Trismegistus, the legendary ancient founder, and Paracelsus, the firebrand reformer of medicine and chymistry, the title page advertises the *Viridarium* as the meeting-ground of ancient Hermetic wisdom and the new Paracelsian art. Lucas Jennis was the Frankfurt publisher behind much of the great early-modern alchemical picture-trade, including the works of Maier and Mylius that Stolcius here anthologises.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made 'cover plate' template: two flanking patron-statues guarding a central title cartouche. Reuse the device as a chapter frontispiece, a codex menu screen, or the doorway to an alchemist's library level.

citations.text: Daniel Stolcius, *Viridarium Chymicum* (1624); cf. Maier, *Atalanta Fugiens*.
Internet Archive scan of the 1624 Frankfurt edition.

entries.title: Viridarium Chymicum — Emblem 1
entries.creator: Daniel Stolcius
entries.summary:
Engraved emblem 1 of Daniel Stolcius's *Viridarium Chymicum* (Frankfurt, 1624) — a copperplate scene of the prima materia and the royal pair, part of an anthology gathered chiefly from Maier's *Atalanta Fugiens* and Mylius's *Philosophia reformata*.

## Iconography
A crowned King and Queen stand in a landscape as the chymical work begins; near them a beast and a furnace-tender mark the rough first matter being made ready. The plate is a small intaglio engraving in the cabinet format of the *Viridarium* — figures set in an open landscape, originally paired in the book with a Latin epigram (not reproduced here). The exact motto for this plate is not recorded in our catalogue, so the scene is described by its alchemical TYPE rather than by an invented inscription.

## Significance
Within the Great Work this image belongs to the meeting of Sol and Luna — fixed and volatile, sulphur and mercury — from whose union the whole opus proceeds. Stolcius, a Bohemian medical student, compiled the *Viridarium* as a visual florilegium of the emblem tradition, digesting the great picture-books of Maier and Mylius into a single 'chymical garden' of 107 plates. The sequence as a whole walks the reader from the rough first matter, through death and washing, to the reddened stone.

## For artists & game designers
A compact, copy-ready emblem: clean line-work, a single legible action, and a landscape stage that drops cleanly into a tarot-style card, a quest icon, or a laboratory backdrop. Pair it with its stage-colour (black, white, or red) to signal progress in an alchemy crafting system.

citations.text: Daniel Stolcius, *Viridarium Chymicum* (1624); cf. Maier, *Atalanta Fugiens*.
Internet Archive scan of the 1624 Frankfurt edition.

The sequence as a whole walks the reader from the rough first matter, through death and washing, to the reddened stone.

The sequence as a whole walks the reader from the rough first matter, through death and washing, to the reddened stone.

## For artists & game designers
A compact, copy-ready emblem: clean line-work, a single legible action, and a landscape stage that drops cleanly into a tarot-style card, a quest icon, or a laboratory backdrop. Pair it with its stage-colour (black, white, or red) to signal progress in an alchemy crafting system.

entries.title: Viridarium Chymicum — Closing Sol-and-Luna Device
entries.creator: Daniel Stolcius
entries.summary:
The closing engraved device of the *Viridarium Chymicum* — an armorial-style emblem crowning the 'Chymical Garden.'

## Iconography
A heraldic cartouche of scrolling acanthus and mantling. A radiant Sun-face surmounted by a crescent Moon rises above a crowned helm; a shield below bears a second Sun-face among orbs and stars. The composition reads as a coat-of-arms built entirely from the luminaries of the alchemical art rather than from family heraldry.

## Significance
Sol and Luna — gold and silver, the fixed and the volatile — are here blazoned as the 'arms' of the whole work, a fitting colophon to an anthology whose every emblem turns on their marriage and separation. Such closing devices were a common flourish in the Jennis alchemical books, sealing the volume as the King's restored sovereignty over the metals.

## For artists & game designers
A drop-in faction crest or end-of-chapter seal: Sun-and-Moon heraldry that signals 'the work is complete' without any text. Recolour the shield to mark different alchemical houses or guilds.

citations.text: Daniel Stolcius, *Viridarium Chymicum* (1624); cf. Maier, *Atalanta Fugiens*.
